title: Certificate Transparency (CT)
description: Public append-only logs of all issued TLS certificates — enables detection of misissued or fraudulent certificates.
fullExplanation: Certificate Transparency (RFC 6962) requires all publicly trusted TLS certificates to be logged in public, append-only CT logs before browsers will trust them. This makes it impossible for a CA to secretly issue a certificate for your domain — any such certificate would appear in the public log. Domain owners can monitor CT logs for unauthorized certificates. Chrome has required CT since 2018. Let's Encrypt logs all certificates automatically.

howItWorks.description: CA issues certificate and submits it to one or more CT logs
CT log returns a Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT) — proof of logging
CA embeds SCT in certificate (or delivers via TLS extension / OCSP)
Browser verifies SCT is present and valid — rejects certs without SCT
Domain owners monitor CT logs for unauthorized certificates

keyFacts: Chrome requires CT for all publicly trusted certs since April 2018
SCT (Signed Certificate Timestamp): cryptographic proof cert was logged
CT logs are append-only — cannot delete or modify entries
crt.sh: public CT log search — find all certs for any domain
Monitors: services like Facebook CT Monitor, Cert Spotter alert on new certs for your domain
Merkle tree: CT logs use cryptographic Merkle trees for efficient proof of inclusion
Google, Cloudflare, DigiCert operate major CT logs
